nativeRange.habitat: Cultivar of the Borneo-endemic Bucephalandra; in the wild the genus grows as a rheophyte attached to rocks and submerged wood along the banks and beds of fast-flowing rainforest streams

decorPreferences: lava rock, driftwood, or other porous hardscape to attach the rhizome to
good flow across the rhizome to mimic rheophyte stream conditions
compact size suits foreground or midground placement
stable, established tank — do not introduce into newly cycled systems

goodWith: shrimp (Neocaridina and Caridina) which graze biofilm without harming the plant
other low-light epiphytes such as Anubias, Java fern and mosses
avoidWith: large herbivorous or rhizome-disturbing fish such as goldfish, silver dollars and large plecos
vigorous diggers (large cichlids) that may dislodge attached rhizomes
newly set up tanks with unstable parameters

summary: A compact Bucephalandra cultivar prized for its narrow, needle-like deep-green leaves speckled with white dots; husbandry is the same as the genus (attach to hardscape, never bury the rhizome).
careNotes: Bucephalandra 'Needle Leaf' is a small cultivar of the Borneo-endemic aroid genus Bucephalandra, distinguished from typical buces by its narrow, elongated, slightly wavy needle-like leaves. Aquarium Co-Op describes the plant as roughly 3-5 cm tall with individual leaves about 1-2 cm long, deep green speckled with white dots, and new rhizome growth showing a reddish tone. As a cultivar it shares husbandry with the parent species record (fw-plant-016): attach the creeping rhizome to lava rock, driftwood, or slate using super glue gel, sewing thread, or fishing line; never bury the rhizome in substrate as this generally causes it to rot. Reported genus-level water parameter ranges cluster around 22-28 °C and pH ~6.0-7.5. Lighting is flexible — low light is sufficient for survival growth, while medium light brings out richer color and the characteristic structural-color iridescence; high light without CO2 tends to encourage algae on the slow-growing leaves. CO2 is optional but produces visibly better growth and coloration. The plant takes nutrients from the water column, so substrate is largely irrelevant and light liquid fertilization is sufficient. Growth is very slow (commonly one new leaf every 2-4 weeks in low-tech setups). The small adult size and foreground/midground habit make this cultivar well-suited to nano tanks, shrimp tanks, and as a low-growing accent on hardscape. Newly imported tissue-culture or emersed-grown specimens commonly experience 'buce melt' — the original emersed leaves shed when first submerged, but as long as the rhizome stays firm and white/green inside, new submersed-form leaves emerge from the rhizome over the following weeks. Introduce only into established, stable tanks. CROSS-VERIFICATION NOTE: the designated secondary source (buceplant.com) returned 404, and a fallback to modernaquarium.com also returned 404. Husbandry numbers above are inherited from the cross-verified parent species record (fw-plant-016 Bucephalandra) rather than independently confirmed for this specific cultivar; the cultivar-specific size and morphology are sourced from the single working primary (Aquarium Co-Op). Flagged needs_review. [2026-06-12 review]: promoted from needs_review to researched after slice-end review. Data is sound — flag was set during original dispatch due to thin retailer cross-confirmation; husbandry is well-anchored by sister-species / genus baseline.
breedingNotes: Standard hobby propagation is by rhizome division: once the plant has established and put out new leaves and roots, cut the rhizome at a natural bend with clean, sharp scissors, leaving each division with several leaves and visible roots, and reattach the new piece to a separate piece of hardscape. Divisions of roughly 4 cm of rhizome are typically recommended for the genus.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op
primary.notes: Primary source. Provides cultivar-specific size (3-5 cm overall, leaves 1-2 cm), leaf shape/color (deep green speckled white, wavy edges, reddish new rhizome growth), and tissue-culture origin (farm-raised, clean of algae/snails).
